summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Patient reports a cough and runny nose.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports feeling sick for the past few days, starting with a cold last night. He describes a barky cough, runny nose, and difficulty sleeping. He reports decreased appetite and irritability. He states he's been giving his child saline mist and Tylenol. The patient reports that his child is also pulling on his ears and has some ear pain. He reports using Baby Vicks and Aveno Shampoo. The patient reports that he gave his child honey nut cheerios this morning. The doctor recommends using a soft brush and baby oil. The patient is sweating.

Findings
The patient's rectal temperature was 100.3 degrees Fahrenheit. The patient reports feeling warm. The patient reports dizziness.

Assessment
Clinical presentation is most consistent with a viral infection and possible sinus infection. The patient suspects a viral infection.

Plan
The patient should let us know if it keeps getting worse. Order shoulder radiographs, initiate physical therapy, and continue acetaminophen as needed. Arrange follow-up in four weeks if symptoms persist. The doctor recommends using nizoral shampoo twice per week. The doctor recommends using head & shoulder shampoo twice per week. The doctor recommends using a soft brush. The doctor recommends using baby oil. The patient can check out.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Randy Gutierrez is a 9-month-old male who is being evaluated today for a cough, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea. He is accompanied by his mother.

Symptoms began yesterday around dinnertime with clear nasal drainage, pulling on his ears bilaterally, and rubbing his nose frequently. He does not have a barky cough or a sore throat. His rectal temperature was 100.3 degrees. The patient did consume a 7 ounce bottle this morning, but mostly denied the next offered bottle and declined cereal. He is irritable and having trouble falling asleep, described “like he is fighting it”.

The patient has been exposed to an older sister who is sick and a cousin who has a sinus infection.

Treatment has included saline nasal spray, Tylenol, baby Vick's, and a humidifier. When the patient aroused this morning, he was warm to the touch. When his sleeper was removed, he was quite sweaty despite the air conditioner being on.

The patient's dandruff is also worsening. The only tried treatment is Aveeno shampoo.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Older sister.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Tylenol PRN.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Temperature 100.3 degrees.

Ears, Eyes, Nose, Throat
Nasal congestion, clear mucus, rhinorrhea. Bilateral pulling of ears, no sore throat or hoarseness.

Respiratory
Positive for cough.

ASSESSMENT

• Viral infection
• Dandruff

PLAN

Viral infection
The patient presents with a viral infection and does not need antibiotics currently. I advised continuation of fluids, rest. Additionally, I suggested trying Zarbee’s for children under 1 years old. If a croup cough develops, the patient will receive a 0.5 teaspoon.

I also provided counseling to the mother to avoid giving honey to the patient for his first year of life due to botulism spores.

Dandruff
The patient is experiencing worsening symptoms of dandruff. I recommended Head and Shoulders shampoo, Selsun Blue, or Nizoral A-D 2 times per week, combined with his usual shampoo. I counseled the mother that she should be careful not to get the shampoo in the patient eyes and to not use the extra strength shampoos. Additionally, he may also use baby oil and a soft brush on the area.

INSTRUCTIONS

Return in one week for a re-check.

sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Patient reports a cough and runny nose.

The patient reports feeling sick for the past few days, starting with a cold last night. He describes a barky cough, runny nose, and difficulty sleeping. Assessment

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports that his child is also pulling on his ears and has some ear pain.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is an observed behavior (pulling on ears) that could reasonably be documented as an exam/observation finding rather than HPI narrative, though it does not substantially distort meaning in HPI.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor recommends using a soft brush and baby oil.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is clearly a treatment recommendation and belongs in the Plan, not in the patient-reported history.
This is a subjective symptom reported by the patient rather than an objective exam or test result; it fits better in HPI, though its placement in Findings is not highly misleading.
Dizziness is a subjective symptom and more appropriate for HPI than for objective Findings, but the misplacement is not severely misleading.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient suspects a viral infection.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is the patient's own impression, not the clinician's diagnostic synthesis; it belongs in HPI as part of the patient's reported history rather than in the clinician Assessment.

section_miscategorizations.text: Order shoulder radiographs, initiate physical therapy, and continue acetaminophen as needed.
section_miscategorizations.reason: These orders are unrelated to the documented respiratory/viral presentation and appear to pertain to a different problem (e.g., shoulder issue). Their inclusion in the Plan for this visit is misleading for the current assessment; they should either be tied to a corresponding assessment or omitted.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor recommends using nizoral shampoo twice per week.
section_miscategorizations.reason: No scalp or dermatologic complaint is documented in HPI or Assessment; introducing this treatment in the Plan without corresponding history/assessment is misleading. Either the related condition should be added to HPI/Assessment, or this recommendation belongs in a different encounter.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor recommends using head & shoulder shampoo twice per week.
section_miscategorizations.reason: As with the Nizoral recommendation, this plan item lacks any supporting symptom or diagnosis in HPI/Assessment, making its placement misleading for this visit's documented problems.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor recommends using a soft brush.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This recommendation is duplicated and already (incorrectly) appears in HPI. Having it both as history and plan without clear context is confusing; the HPI instance should be moved to Plan, and only one clear Plan statement retained.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor recommends using baby oil.
section_miscategorizations.reason: As with the soft brush recommendation, this duplicates a recommendation embedded in HPI. The HPI occurrence should be moved to Plan to avoid mixing clinician instructions into the history narrative.
